title: Dog Urinating Blood Clots: Causes and Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is urinating blood clots, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn the causes and why you must seek immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Blood clots in dog urine are a critical veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to prevent life-threatening urinary blockages. Capturing a photo of the urine can significantly assist the triage team in assessing the severity of the bleeding.

What does it mean if I see blood clots in my dog's urine?

Observing blood clots in your dog's urine, a condition known as gross hematuria, is a serious clinical sign. It indicates that bleeding in the urinary tract—which includes the kidneys, ureters, bladder, or urethra—is significant enough for the blood to pool and coagulate. This is different from simple discoloration and often suggests an acute or advanced underlying issue.

Is finding blood clots in dog urine a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for blood clots in urine is high; you should contact an emergency veterinarian or your primary clinic immediately.
  • Beyond the underlying cause of the bleeding, the clots themselves can lodge in the urethra and cause a life-threatening urinary blockage.
  • A blockage prevents your dog from emptying their bladder, which is a critical medical emergency requiring rapid intervention.

Why should I take a photo of the urine for the triage staff?

  • Taking a clear photo of the urine and clots allows veterinary teams to quickly assess the volume of blood loss.
  • A visual reference helps the triage staff determine how quickly your dog needs to be stabilized upon arrival.
  • Photos assist the veterinarian in narrowing down potential causes during the initial diagnostic exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hematuria, dysuria, stranguria, and pollakiuria are common clinical signs associated with urinary tract issues. Urethral obstruction is an emergency, leading to uremia within 36-48 hours, and potentially causing depression, anorexia, vomiting, diarrhea, dehydration, coma, and death within 48-72 hours. Neoplasms of the bladder and urethra, as well as uroliths throughout the urinary tract, can cause hematuria and potential obstruction. A complete radiographic examination, including contrast studies or ultrasonography, is indicated to identify radiolucent calculi. Urinalysis, including microscopic examination for crystals and bacterial culture and sensitivity testing, is critical.

Chapter: Urology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1527)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of blood clots in dog urine?
faqs.answer: Common causes include severe urinary tract infections, bladder stones, trauma, bladder tumors, or ingestion of toxins like rodenticide which affects blood clotting.

faqs.question: My dog seems fine otherwise; can I wait to see the vet?
faqs.answer: No. Even if your dog is acting normally, the presence of clots indicates significant internal bleeding or inflammation that can lead to a sudden, painful blockage or systemic crisis.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for straining to urinate, frequent attempts to pee with little output, lethargy, pale gums, or a painful abdomen. These are signs of a critical situation.
